::: {.content-hidden}
Copyright (C) 2026 Harald Pretl and co-authors (harald.pretl@jku.at)

Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
You may obtain a copy of the License at

    http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0

Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
limitations under the License.
:::

# Spurs, Crosstalk and Self-Interference {#sec-spurs}

In practical RFICs, unwanted tones and unintended coupling paths often limit the achievable dynamic range (expressed for example as SNR or EVM) or cause performance issues (like out-of-band emissions in TX or RX blocking) that render standards-compliant operation impossible. In modern carrier-aggregation transceivers, the integration of several TX and RX paths together with multiple local oscillators on a single die turns this into a major source of concern [@Sadjina.2020].

A *spur* (short for "spurious tone") is a discrete spectral line at an unwanted frequency. A common metric is the spur level relative to the wanted carrier, expressed in dBc:

$$
L_\mathrm{spur} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{V_\mathrm{spur}}{V_\mathrm{carrier}} \right)\;\text{dBc}.
$$

*Crosstalk* is undesired signal transfer from an aggressor node to a victim node through electric, magnetic, substrate, or supply coupling [@Verghese.1995]. Coupling mainly happens through parasitic capacitances from high voltage-swing nodes, or inductively from high-current loops. Shared traces (like supply and ground connections) can also lead to ohmic coupling.

A first-order estimate for capacitive crosstalk from aggressor to victim is

$$
\left|\frac{V_\mathrm{victim}}{V_\mathrm{aggressor}}\right| \approx \omega C_\mathrm{c} \left|Z_\mathrm{victim}\right|,
$$

showing that coupling tends to worsen at higher frequency and higher victim impedance. In high-performance RFICs, crosstalk and spur levels on the order of $-80$ dBc or lower are often required to meet system-level specifications.

## Coupling Paths {#sec-spurs-mechanisms}

@fig-trx-coupling shows the main on-chip coupling paths in a typical TRX architecture. The following discussion uses the crosstalk nomenclature introduced in [@Sadjina.2020].

{{< include /content/spurs/_fig_trx_coupling.qmd >}}

- TX-to-RX coupling through the RFIC, duplexer and shared front-end elements ("TX leakage") can cause issues such as gain desensitization, reciprocal mixing, receive-band noise from the TX, and IM2/IM3 distortion in the RX [@Aparin.2006].
- RX VCO coupling ("RX LO leakage") from the RX VCO/PLL into the LNA/mixer can cause increased IM2 products and LO radiation.
- LO-to-LO coupling through the LO distribution network can cause reciprocal mixing due to LO spurs in RX, or OOB emissions on TX. When the two oscillators run at nearby frequencies, injection pulling between the VCOs can also degrade the spectral purity of both LOs [@Razavi.2004].
- TX VCO coupling ("TX LO leakage") from the TX VCO/PLL into the PA can cause increased LO leakage and EVM degradation.

## Synthesizer Spurs {#sec-spurs-pll}

In addition to the inter-block coupling discussed above, the frequency synthesizer itself is a notorious source of *intra-PLL* spurs (see also @sec-pll). The most relevant mechanisms are:

- **Reference spurs** at integer multiples of the reference frequency, $f_\mathrm{spur} = f_\mathrm{LO} \pm k\, f_\mathrm{ref}$, $k \in \mathbb{N}^+$. They are caused by charge-pump mismatch and leakage, finite isolation of the loop-filter node, and supply or substrate coupling at $f_\mathrm{ref}$. Their amplitude is set by how strongly the loop filter rejects components at $f_\mathrm{ref}$ relative to the loop bandwidth.
- **Fractional-$N$ spurs** generated by the periodicity and quantization noise of the $\Delta\Sigma$ modulator that controls the divider (see @sec-pll-delta-sigma). They typically appear as families of tones around $f_\mathrm{LO}$ at offsets related to the fractional ratio. Higher-order modulators and dithering reduce their level at the cost of increased close-in noise.
- **Divider and digital-switching spurs** at sub-harmonics of $f_\mathrm{LO}$ and at digital clock frequencies, coupling into the VCO through the supply and substrate.
- **Digital spurs** originating from TDC or DCO nonlinearities in all-digital PLLs, or from digital blocks in the vicinity of the VCO.

Once these spurs appear on the LO, they translate directly into the receive band through reciprocal mixing of a strong blocker, or into the transmit spectrum through the upconversion mixer. If these spurs are inside the channel bandwidth, they cause EVM degradation and may violate spectral mask requirements.

## Spur and Crosstalk Mitigation {#sec-spurs-mitigation}

Mitigation of spurs and crosstalk is a complex topic that requires careful design of the entire system, including the architecture, floorplan, circuit design, layout, and packaging. Some common techniques are summarized [here](https://iic-jku.github.io/analog-circuit-design/aicd.html#rules-for-good-mixed-signal-and-rf-circuits). In addition, inductor-to-inductor coupling has to be considered and may require quadrupole inductors [@Poon.2009] or patterned ground shields [@Yue.1998].

Substrate and supply coupling deserve special attention because they couple every block to every other block through a common low-impedance node [@Verghese.1995]. Effective measures include placing sensitive analog and RF blocks in dedicated deep-n-well or triple-well pockets, surrounding aggressors and victims with substrate guard rings, separating supply domains for digital, analog, RF, and LO blocks, and using on-chip and on-package decoupling so that switching currents do not modulate the supply seen by the VCO or LNA. A careful floorplan that keeps high-swing TX nodes, LO distribution, and digital clocks physically far from sensitive RX inputs and VCO inductors is usually more effective than any post-layout fix.

If supply decoupling is insufficient, series or shunt supply regulators and additional low-pass filters for supply and reference lines can help to reduce supply-bound coupling [@ElNozahi.2010]. However, these measures increase the power consumption and area of the RFIC, and may not be feasible in all applications.

Where possible, mitigation should also be performed by digital signal processing of the RX or TX baseband signals, for example digital predistortion, digital IM2 cancellation [@Gebhard.2019], or digital self-interference cancellation [@Sadjina.2020]. However, these techniques require additional hardware and power, and are not always feasible.
